nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats, slopes, and lagoons at 1–30 m depth; typically found resting on substrate or encrusting along lower reef surfaces with moderate light and flow

decorPreferences: sand bed or low rockwork substrate placement
stable flat surface for plating growth
ample open space around colony (10+ cm clearance from neighbors)
avoid burying in fine sand — crushed coral or rubble preferred substrate

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, Reef Roids, or nano-plankton blends tends to support growth and coloration. Foods may be delivered by pipette or turkey baster. Pellets and sinking foods have also been reported as accepted.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
other Lithophyllon without adequate spacing for colony expansion
direct high-flow powerheads aimed at the colony

summary: Lithophyllon is a genus of LPS plating corals in family Fungiidae, closely related to Fungia disc corals despite their encrusting plate morphology. Generally considered intermediate care; prefers moderate light, gentle-to-moderate flow, and lower placement near the substrate.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: Despite a plating or encrusting chalice-like growth form, Lithophyllon belongs to family Fungiidae (order Scleractinia) — the same family as Fungia mushroom/disc corals — rather than Lobophylliidae (where true chalice corals like Echinophyllia are placed). This makes Lithophyllon a genetically closer relative to free-living plate corals (Fungia, Cycloseris, Heliofungia) than to Echinophyllia. In the aquarium trade, Lithophyllon is sometimes marketed as a chalice coral due to superficial morphological similarities, which can cause confusion. The genus Lithophyllon encompasses six species recognized by the World Register of Marine Species; the most commonly encountered in the trade are L. undulatum and L. repanda. Some specimens formerly sold as Echinophyllia 'chalice' may actually be Lithophyllon, particularly encrusting multi-mouth forms.

L. repanda is capable of very limited mobility in the wild, occasionally repositioning on substrate — a trait shared with other Fungiidae members.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep under moderate light, commonly cited around 75–150 PAR at coral depth. Sources consistently recommend lower to moderate intensity, with care taken when acclimating specimens to higher light conditions. New arrivals should be started in shaded areas and gradually moved to target placement over 2–4 weeks to avoid bleaching. Blue-spectrum lighting tends to enhance fluorescent coloration morph expression.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is broadly recommended. Flow should be sufficient to keep the ornate skeletal surface free of detritus but not so strong as to stress tissue. Avoid sustained laminar flow directed at the colony.

Aggression: Lithophyllon tends to have a fairly powerful sting but shorter tentacles compared to Echinophyllia chalices — sources describe tentacles as short and not typical long sweepers, though the sting can damage neighboring coral tissue on contact. Maintaining 5–10 cm minimum spacing from other corals is advisable, with extra clearance as the colony expands.

Placement: Typically placed on or near the sand bed, simulating the natural resting position of wild Fungiidae corals. A flat, stable surface is ideal; the colony encrusts outward and can plate significantly over time. Chunky rubble or crushed coral substrate is preferred over fine sand, which can accumulate around the colony and cause tissue recession.

Water chemistry: Stability of alkalinity is particularly important; sources note Lithophyllon is sensitive to alkalinity swings. Nitrate levels of 5–20 ppm and phosphate under 0.1 ppm are generally considered workable; ultra-low nutrient systems may cause color loss.

Conservation: L. repanda is rated Least Concern by IUCN; L. undulatum is listed as Near Threatened. The genus is listed on CITES Appendix II. Most trade specimens are captive-grown frags.

Morph diversity: Lithophyllon is available in a wide range of color morphs in the hobby, including named variants such as C3PO, Immolation, Cheetah, Kiwi, Ice Sheet, and Dragons Blood, among others. These are color morphs of the same genus and do not require different care.

Cross-source temperature disagreement: Sources ranged from 74–84°F (23–29°C) across references. The filed range of 24–27°C represents the averaged consensus of more specialist sources; some sources noting 82–84°F upper bounds may reflect broader tolerance rather than optimal husbandry.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters; frags of 2–3 cm typically encrust and grow on rubble or frag plugs within a few weeks under stable conditions. Coral dipping before introduction is recommended to prevent hitchhiker pests. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: AquaLife Support – Lithophyllon Coral
primary.notes: Primary source used for lighting preference (<100 PAR), flow (random gentle), placement (bottom), temperature (74–84°F), salinity (1.024–1.026), feeding (meaty foods), native range (Indian and Pacific oceans, parts of Borneo), and aggression notes (powerful sting, short tentacles).

additional.name: TankFacts – Kiwi Litho (Lithophyllon)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Fungiidae, CITES Appendix II, Near Threatened), placement (bottom), flow (moderate), lighting (moderate), water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament. Used as secondary cross-verification.

additional.name: Vivid Aquariums – Strawberry Lithophyllon Coral
additional.notes: Provided water parameters: temperature 75–80°F, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 420–440 ppm, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, magnesium 1260–1350 ppm. Listed PAR 250–450 and flow as moderate-to-high — these higher PAR figures appear to be outliers inconsistent with specialist sources and the task brief consensus; the lower PAR range from specialist sources was adopted instead.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Plate Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked Fungiidae taxonomy, Pacific/Australian/Indonesian native range, lighting (50–150 PAR; 50 PAR minimum), flow (medium to low), placement (substrate/bottom), feeding (≤3× weekly, meaty foods), calcium ~425 ppm, alkalinity ~8 dKH, magnesium ~1350 ppm. Describes corals as easy-to-moderate in care; confirmed preferred substrate as medium-chunky rubble/crushed coral over fine sand.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Lithophyllon repanda
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Fungiidae, order Scleractinia), distribution (Indo-Pacific: Gulf of Aden, Red Sea, Indian Ocean, Australia, East China Sea, Japan, western and central Pacific), depth range (1–30 m), colony size (up to 30 cm diameter, up to 23.5 cm maximum recorded), and conservation status (IUCN Least Concern, CITES Appendix II).
